message: URGENT: Your Chase account has been locked. Verify now at chase-secure-login.com or lose access permanently within 12 hours.

red_flags: Fake urgency with 12-hour deadline
Suspicious domain (chase-secure-login.com is not chase.com)
Threat of permanent account loss
Generic greeting instead of your name
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing email trying to steal your Chase login credentials. The link goes to a fake website that looks like Chase but is not. Chase will never email you with urgent deadlines threatening to lock your account.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Delete this message
Log into chase.com directly to check your account
Call Chase at 1-800-935-9935 if concerned
analysis.safe_alternatives: Open your browser and type chase.com directly. Log in there to verify your account status.

message: Hello dear, I am Barrister Ahmed from Nigeria. My late client left $12.5 million with no next of kin. I need a foreign partner to claim the funds. You will receive 40%. Reply with your bank details.

red_flags: Classic Nigerian advance-fee scam pattern
Stranger offering millions of dollars
Asks for bank details upfront
Too good to be true offer
Claims about deceased client with no heirs
Will eventually ask you to pay various fees
analysis.explanation: This is one of the oldest scam types on the internet. There is no money, no deceased client, and no inheritance. If you respond, they will ask you to pay various fees (taxes, legal fees, transfer fees) that keep getting larger. You will never receive any money.
recommended_actions: Do NOT respond
Do NOT share any personal or banking information
Mark as spam and delete
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: No legitimate financial opportunity comes from a stranger emailing you about millions of dollars. Delete and forget.

message: Hi Mom, I lost my phone and this is my new number. Can you send me $500 through Zelle? I'm in trouble and need it right now. Please don't call my old number.

red_flags: Claims to be family member with new number
Urgently requests money via Zelle (hard to reverse)
Tells you NOT to call the real number to verify
Vague about what the trouble is
Zelle payments are instant and irreversible
analysis.explanation: This is a family impersonation scam. The person texting you is NOT your child. Scammers send this message to thousands of people hoping some will have children and panic. They specifically tell you not to call the old number because your real child would tell you it is a scam.
recommended_actions: Do NOT send money
Call your child at their known phone number immediately
If you can't reach them, contact another family member to verify
Do not continue texting with this number
Report the number to your carrier
analysis.safe_alternatives: Call your child directly at the number you have saved. If they truly lost their phone, they can contact you through other means like email or a friend's phone.

message: Your prescription from CVS Pharmacy is ready for pickup at the Main St location. Refill #RX-4471982.

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard prescription ready notification from CVS Pharmacy. It includes a specific pharmacy location and refill number, which is consistent with how CVS sends these messages.
recommended_actions: If you have prescriptions at CVS, this is likely legitimate
Verify by calling your CVS pharmacy directly if unsure
Check the CVS app for your prescription status
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can always verify by opening the CVS app or calling your pharmacy location directly.

message: ALERT: Someone just used your debit card for $892.47 at Best Buy in Miami, FL. If this was NOT you, reply STOP to block your card immediately.

red_flags: Creates panic about unauthorized charges
Asks you to reply to the text (engagement trap)
Real banks don't ask you to reply STOP to block cards
Designed to get you to call back or share information
Specific dollar amount to seem more believable
analysis.explanation: This is a smishing (SMS phishing) scam. While it mentions a specific amount to seem real, your bank will never ask you to reply to a text message to block your card. If you reply, they will try to get your card number, PIN, or other personal details to steal from you.
recommended_actions: Do NOT reply to this text
Call the number on the back of your actual debit card
Log into your bank's app to check for real transactions
Report the text to 7726 (SPAM)
Block this phone number
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are worried about unauthorized charges, call the phone number printed on the back of your physical debit card. Never use a phone number or link from a suspicious text.

message: You've been selected for a $750 Costco gift card! Take our 30-second survey to claim. Limited time offer for loyal members.

red_flags: You were not randomly selected for anything
Survey scams harvest personal information
Costco does not give away $750 gift cards via random texts
Creates urgency with limited time
Will likely ask for credit card to pay for shipping
analysis.explanation: This is a survey scam. The survey will collect your personal information, and at the end they will ask for a credit card number to pay for shipping the gift card. There is no gift card. Costco does not run promotions like this.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click any links
Do NOT take the survey
Delete the message
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: Costco's real promotions are on their official website costco.com and in their app. They never text random people about gift card giveaways.

message: MICROSOFT ALERT: Your computer has been compromised! Call 1-888-555-0147 immediately to speak with a Microsoft certified technician. Do NOT shut down your computer. Error code: DW6VB36.

red_flags: Microsoft does not send pop-up alerts with phone numbers
Fake error code designed to look technical
Tells you NOT to shut down to keep you panicked
The phone number connects to a scam call center
Will ask for remote access to your computer or payment
analysis.explanation: This is a tech support scam. Microsoft will never show you a pop-up asking you to call a phone number. If you call, the person will pretend to be from Microsoft and ask you to give them remote access to your computer. Then they will either install malware, steal your files, or demand hundreds of dollars for fake repairs.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
Close the browser tab or window showing this message
If the message won't close, press Ctrl+Alt+Delete and end the browser process
Run a legitimate antivirus scan (Windows Defender is built in and free)
Never give remote access to someone who contacts you
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you genuinely think your computer has a problem, take it to a local repair shop you trust, or contact Microsoft support through their official website support.microsoft.com.

message: Hey beautiful, I know we just met on Hinge last week, but I feel like we have something truly special. I'm a petroleum engineer working offshore right now, so I can't video chat. But I have an investment opportunity that could set us both up -- my buddy runs a crypto trading platform that guarantees 30% monthly returns. Can you start with just $500?

red_flags: Met recently online and already professing deep feelings
Cannot video chat due to convenient excuse (working offshore)
Introduces investment opportunity early in relationship
Guaranteed returns are a hallmark of fraud (30% monthly is impossible)
Asks you to invest money with someone you have never met
Classic pig butchering scam pattern
analysis.explanation: This is a combination romance and investment scam, sometimes called pig butchering. The person builds a fake romantic connection to gain your trust, then steers you toward a fraudulent investment platform. The platform will show fake profits to get you to invest more, but when you try to withdraw, your money will be gone. No legitimate investment guarantees 30% monthly returns.
recommended_actions: Do NOT send any money
Do NOT invest in any platform they recommend
Insist on a video call -- if they always refuse, it is a scam
Report the profile to Hinge
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
Talk to a trusted friend or family member about this
analysis.safe_alternatives: If someone you met online asks you for money or pushes you toward investments, that is a scam. Real relationships do not come with financial requests attached. Use only regulated brokerages like Fidelity, Schwab, or Vanguard for investing.

message: This is the IRS. You owe $4,782.00 in back taxes. A warrant has been issued for your arrest. To avoid arrest, call 202-555-0198 immediately and pay via gift cards or wire transfer. Case #IRS-2026-38847.

red_flags: The IRS never calls or texts about tax debts -- they send letters by mail
Threatening arrest for unpaid taxes is a scare tactic
No legitimate government agency accepts gift cards as payment
Wire transfers are irreversible -- a favorite of scammers
Fake case number to appear official
High-pressure demand for immediate payment
analysis.explanation: This is a government impersonation scam. The IRS will never call, text, or email you demanding immediate payment. They always send official letters through the US Postal Service first. And no government agency in the world accepts gift cards as payment. This is designed to scare you into sending money before you have time to think clearly.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
Do NOT buy gift cards or send wire transfers
Hang up or delete the message immediately
If you have real tax concerns, call the IRS directly at 1-800-829-1040
Report this to the Treasury Inspector General at 1-800-366-4484
analysis.safe_alternatives: The real IRS will always contact you first by physical mail. If you owe taxes, you can check your status at irs.gov or call 1-800-829-1040.

message: Congratulations! You've been hired as a Remote Quality Inspector for Nexus Global Corp. Salary: $65/hr, flexible hours, work from home. To begin onboarding, we need you to purchase a laptop and office supplies using our company funds. We'll send you a check for $3,500 -- deposit it and use the money to buy equipment from our approved vendor.

red_flags: Job offer you never applied for
Unusually high pay for the described work ($65/hr)
Asking you to deposit a check and spend the money
The check will bounce after you have already spent real money
No legitimate company asks new hires to buy their own equipment with a mailed check
No interview process described
analysis.explanation: This is a fake check employment scam. They will send you a check that looks real, and your bank might even let you deposit it. But after a few days, the check will bounce, and your bank will take the money back from your account. By then, you will have already sent your real money to the scammer's approved vendor. You will be out thousands of dollars.
recommended_actions: Do NOT deposit any check from someone you do not know
Do NOT purchase anything on behalf of this company
Never spend money from a check until it has fully cleared (which takes 2+ weeks)
Report this to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
Search for the company name online to confirm it is real
analysis.safe_alternatives: Real employers never ask you to deposit checks and buy equipment. They ship you what you need or reimburse you through payroll. Look for jobs on legitimate sites like Indeed, LinkedIn, or company career pages.

message: URGENT: Children in war-torn South Sudan are starving. The Global Relief Children's Fund needs your help NOW. Every minute you wait, another child dies. Donate at globalreliefchildrensfund.org. We accept gift cards and cryptocurrency.

red_flags: Extreme emotional manipulation and urgency
Unfamiliar charity name with no track record
Accepts gift cards (no real charity does this)
Accepts cryptocurrency (unusual for legitimate charities)
Pressure to donate immediately without researching
Domain name not associated with any known relief organization
analysis.explanation: This is a charity scam that exploits real human suffering to steal your money. Legitimate charities do not accept gift cards or pressure you to donate immediately. The organization name sounds official but is not a registered nonprofit. Scammers often set up fake charities around real disasters and conflicts to take advantage of generous people.
recommended_actions: Do NOT donate to this organization
Do NOT click the link
Check any charity at charitynavigator.org or give.org before donating
If you want to help South Sudan, donate to known organizations like UNICEF, Red Cross, or Doctors Without Borders
Report fake charities to the FTC
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want to help, donate directly at unicef.org, redcross.org, or doctorswithoutborders.org. Always verify a charity at charitynavigator.org before giving money.

message: USPS: Your package could not be delivered due to an incomplete address. Please update your shipping info at usps-redelivery-update.com to reschedule. Tracking: 9400111899223847650321

red_flags: Suspicious domain (usps-redelivery-update.com is not usps.com)
USPS sends physical notices for delivery failures, not texts with links
The link will ask for personal information or credit card details
Tracking number format looks plausible but leads to a fake site
Preys on people expecting packages
analysis.explanation: This is a package delivery scam. The link goes to a fake website that looks like USPS but will steal your personal information or credit card number. Real USPS delivery failure notices come as physical slips left at your door, not text messages with links to unfamiliar websites.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Go to usps.com directly and enter the tracking number there
If the tracking number is fake, this confirms it is a scam
Report the text to 7726 (SPAM)
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are expecting a package, go directly to usps.com and enter your real tracking number. You can also call USPS at 1-800-275-8777.

message: Your Apple ID has been disabled for security reasons. Verify your identity within 24 hours or your account and all purchases will be permanently deleted. Click here: apple-id-verify-secure.com

red_flags: Fake domain (apple-id-verify-secure.com is not apple.com)
Threat of permanent deletion to create panic
24-hour deadline creates artificial urgency
Apple never threatens to delete your purchases
Generic message with no personal details
analysis.explanation: This is an account verification phishing scam. The link leads to a fake Apple website designed to steal your Apple ID, password, and possibly your credit card information. Apple will never threaten to delete your account and purchases over email or text. If there were a real issue, you would see it when you log into your Apple account directly.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Go to appleid.apple.com directly to check your account
If you entered your info on the fake site, change your Apple ID password immediately
Enable two-factor authentication on your Apple account
Report phishing to Apple at reportphishing@apple.com
analysis.safe_alternatives: Go to appleid.apple.com directly in your browser, or open Settings on your iPhone and tap your name at the top to check your Apple ID status.

message: Hey, this is your grandson Tyler. I'm in jail and I need bail money. Please don't tell Mom and Dad, I'm so embarrassed. Can you go to Walgreens and buy $2,000 in Google Play gift cards and read me the numbers? My lawyer said this is the fastest way to post bail.

red_flags: Claims to be a grandchild in trouble
Asks for gift cards as payment (no bail system accepts gift cards)
Tells you to keep it secret from other family members
Specific store mentioned to make it seem actionable
Emotional manipulation using shame and urgency
No legitimate legal process involves Google Play gift cards
analysis.explanation: This is a grandparent scam combined with a gift card scam. No one has ever posted bail with Google Play gift cards. The scammer is pretending to be your grandchild and is counting on your love and worry to make you act before thinking. They tell you to keep it secret so no one can talk you out of it.
recommended_actions: Do NOT buy any gift cards
Hang up and call your grandchild at their real number
Call your grandchild's parents to verify the story
No jail or lawyer accepts gift cards -- this is always a scam
Report to local police and the FTC
analysis.safe_alternatives: Call your grandchild directly using the number you already have. If they do not answer, call their parents. If someone really were in jail, a lawyer would contact you through proper channels, never by asking for gift cards.

message: Need cash fast? EZ Quick Loans approves everyone! No credit check, no income verification. Get $5,000-$50,000 deposited today! Just pay a small $299 processing fee upfront. Bad credit? No problem! Apply now at ezquickloans.net

analysis.scam_type: Predatory lending / Advance fee loan scam
red_flags: No credit check and no income verification is not how real lending works
Upfront fee required before receiving the loan
Legitimate lenders deduct fees from the loan, they never ask for money first
Too-good-to-be-true loan amounts with no qualifications
Targets people in financial distress
Unregulated lender with no verifiable credentials
analysis.explanation: This is an advance fee loan scam. They promise you a large loan regardless of your credit, but first you have to pay a processing fee. After you pay, the loan never comes, and they may keep asking for more fees. Real lenders never guarantee approval without checking your credit, and they never ask for upfront fees.
recommended_actions: Do NOT pay any upfront fees for a loan
Do NOT share your bank account or Social Security number
Check if any lender is registered with your state's banking regulator
If you need a loan, visit your local bank or credit union
Report to the FTC and your state attorney general
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you need a loan, visit a local bank or credit union. You can also check rates at legitimate online lenders like SoFi, LendingClub, or Prosper. Real lenders never charge fees before giving you money.

message: URGENT -- Act now! Bitcoin is about to explode to $500K. Our exclusive AI trading bot guarantees 10x returns in 30 days. Minimum investment $1,000. Join our VIP Telegram group for the link. Only 50 spots left!

red_flags: Guaranteed returns (impossible in any market)
Extreme urgency and artificial scarcity (only 50 spots)
Specific price prediction with no basis
AI trading bot claims with no verifiable track record
Directs to Telegram (common scam platform for unregulated schemes)
No registered investment advisor or SEC filing
analysis.explanation: This is a crypto investment scam. Nobody can guarantee 10x returns in 30 days, and anyone who claims to is lying. These schemes typically work by collecting your money through an unregulated platform. They might show fake profits on a dashboard, but when you try to withdraw, you will find the money is gone. The Telegram group is used because it is harder for authorities to trace.
recommended_actions: Do NOT invest any money
Do NOT join the Telegram group
Block and report the sender
Only invest through regulated brokerages (Fidelity, Schwab, Coinbase for crypto)
Report to the SEC at sec.gov/tcr
If you already sent money, contact your bank immediately
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want to invest in Bitcoin or crypto, use a regulated exchange like Coinbase, Kraken, or Gemini. Never invest based on guaranteed returns or pressure from strangers.

message: Your Amazon order #112-4938271-6647832 has shipped! Estimated delivery: Thursday, April 9. Track your package: amazon.com/gp/your-account/order-history

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard Amazon shipping confirmation. The order number format matches Amazon's pattern, and the tracking link points to the real amazon.com domain. If you recently ordered something from Amazon, this is most likely a legitimate notification.
recommended_actions: If you recently placed an Amazon order, this is likely real
You can verify by logging into amazon.com and checking Your Orders
If you did not order anything, log into your Amazon account to check for unauthorized orders
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can always check your order status by going directly to amazon.com and clicking Your Orders.

message: Reminder: Your appointment with Dr. Sarah Chen is scheduled for Tuesday, April 8 at 2:30 PM. Please arrive 15 minutes early. To reschedule, call 770-555-0123. Reply C to confirm.

analysis.explanation: This is a standard medical appointment reminder. It includes a specific doctor name, date, time, and a phone number to reschedule. Medical offices commonly send these automated reminders. The request to reply C to confirm is standard practice for appointment confirmation systems.
recommended_actions: If you have an appointment with this doctor, confirm or reschedule as needed
If you do not recognize the doctor, call the number to clarify or ignore the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are unsure, you can call your doctor's office directly using the number from their website or your previous paperwork.

message: Your Wells Fargo checking account statement is ready. Log in to view your April statement. wellsfargo.com/statements

red_flags: Verify the link actually goes to wellsfargo.com by hovering over it before clicking
analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate bank statement notification from Wells Fargo. Banks routinely send monthly statement-ready notifications. The link appears to go to the real Wells Fargo domain. However, it is always good practice to go to the bank's website directly rather than clicking email links.
recommended_actions: If you have a Wells Fargo account, this is likely legitimate
As a precaution, type wellsfargo.com directly in your browser rather than clicking the link
Check your statement for any unfamiliar transactions
analysis.safe_alternatives: Type wellsfargo.com directly in your browser and log in to view your statement. This avoids any risk from clicking links.

message: Hi, this is Jessica from your company's HR department. We're updating our payroll system and need you to verify your direct deposit information. Please reply with your bank routing number and account number so we can make sure your next paycheck goes through.

red_flags: HR departments do not request banking info via text or email
No specific company name mentioned
Asking for sensitive financial details through an insecure channel
Creates urgency by implying your paycheck is at risk
Legitimate payroll changes are done through secure HR portals
analysis.explanation: This is a business email compromise (BEC) scam. A scammer is pretending to be from your company's HR department to steal your bank account information. Real HR departments would never ask for your routing and account numbers through text or email. They use secure internal systems for payroll changes.
recommended_actions: Do NOT reply with any banking information
Contact your actual HR department using the phone number or email from your company directory
Forward this message to your IT security team
If you already replied, contact your bank immediately to secure your accounts
analysis.safe_alternatives: If your company genuinely needs to update payroll information, they will direct you to a secure HR portal or have you fill out paperwork in person. Always verify by calling HR directly.

message: Thank you for your purchase at Target. $47.82 charged to your Visa ending in 4821. View receipt: target.com/receipts

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard digital receipt from Target. It includes a specific charge amount and the last four digits of a card, which is consistent with how retailers send purchase confirmations. If you recently shopped at Target and recognize the amount, this is most likely legitimate.
recommended_actions: If you shopped at Target recently and the amount looks right, this is legitimate
If you did not make this purchase, check your credit card statement and contact your card issuer
You can verify by logging into your Target account at target.com
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your credit card statement to confirm the charge matches. You can also view receipts in the Target app if you have a Target Circle account.

message: Your Netflix subscription will renew on April 12 for $15.49. No action needed. Manage your plan at netflix.com/account.

analysis.explanation: This is a standard Netflix subscription renewal notice. It includes the renewal date and price, and the link points to the official netflix.com domain. Netflix regularly sends these notifications before charging your card. No action is required unless you want to change your plan.
recommended_actions: If you have Netflix, this is a normal renewal reminder
If you want to change your plan, go to netflix.com/account
If you do not have Netflix, someone may be using your payment method -- check your bank statement
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can manage your Netflix subscription at any time by going directly to netflix.com and logging in.

message: Hi Corey, this is Dr. Kim's office confirming your teeth cleaning on Wednesday at 10 AM. Please do not eat or drink for 30 min before. See you then!

analysis.explanation: This is a standard dental appointment confirmation. It uses your first name, mentions a specific procedure, date, and time, and provides practical instructions. Dental offices commonly send these types of messages. Nothing here is suspicious.
recommended_actions: If you have a dental appointment scheduled, this is legitimate
Follow the fasting instructions provided
If you need to reschedule, call the office back
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you do not recognize this dentist, simply call back using their listed phone number to clarify.

message: A]ert: Your Social Security number has been suspended due to suspicious activity. Call the Social Security Administration immediately at 1-800-555-0199 to reactivate it. Failure to respond will result in a federal arrest warrant. Reference #SSA-2026-4492.

red_flags: Social Security numbers cannot be suspended
The SSA never calls threatening arrest
Fake reference number to appear official
The phone number is not the real SSA number
Misspelled word in Alert (likely to bypass spam filters)
Government agencies do not demand immediate phone calls
analysis.explanation: This is a government impersonation scam. Your Social Security number cannot be suspended -- that is not a real thing. The Social Security Administration does not call people threatening arrest, and they certainly do not do it over text. If there were a genuine issue with your Social Security account, the SSA would send you a letter in the mail.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
Your Social Security number is fine -- it cannot be suspended
If you have real concerns about your SSA account, call 1-800-772-1213 (the real SSA number)
Report this scam to the SSA Office of the Inspector General at oig.ssa.gov
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: The real Social Security Administration can be reached at 1-800-772-1213 or at ssa.gov. They will never threaten you with arrest over the phone.

message: Hey there! I saw your profile and you seem really interesting. I'm a successful forex trader living in Singapore and I'd love to teach you my system. I've turned $500 into $50,000 in 6 months using a method that takes just 15 minutes a day. Want me to show you? No cost to learn, you just need to open an account on this platform I use.

red_flags: Unsolicited contact from a stranger offering financial advice
Unrealistic returns ($500 to $50,000 in 6 months)
Claims it takes minimal effort (15 minutes a day)
Directs you to a specific platform (likely unregulated or fake)
No verifiable credentials or regulatory registration
Too good to be true financial claims
analysis.explanation: This is a forex trading scam. The person will direct you to an unregulated trading platform where you deposit money. The platform will show fake profits on screen, but when you try to withdraw your money, you will discover it is gone. Real forex trading is extremely risky and most people lose money. Nobody turns $500 into $50,000 in six months consistently.
recommended_actions: Do NOT open any accounts on their recommended platform
Do NOT send any money
Block this person
If you are interested in trading, use only regulated brokers
Report to the CFTC at cftc.gov/complaint
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want to learn about trading, use established educational resources and only trade through brokers registered with the SEC or CFTC, such as TD Ameritrade, Interactive Brokers, or Fidelity.

message: FedEx: Your package is being held at the sorting facility because the customs fee of $3.95 has not been paid. Pay now to avoid return to sender: fedex-customs-payment.com/pay

red_flags: Fake domain (fedex-customs-payment.com is not fedex.com)
Small dollar amount designed to seem harmless
FedEx does not collect customs fees via text message links
Creates urgency with threat of returning the package
The site will collect your credit card information
analysis.explanation: This is a package delivery phishing scam. The small fee of $3.95 is designed to seem harmless so you will enter your credit card information without thinking twice. But the website is fake, and once you enter your card details, the scammers can use them for much larger charges. FedEx handles customs fees through official channels, not random text messages.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Do NOT enter any payment information
If you are expecting a FedEx package, track it at fedex.com directly
Report to FedEx at abuse@fedex.com
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you genuinely owe customs fees on a FedEx package, FedEx will either collect payment at delivery or contact you through their official fedex.com website and app.

message: Your Google password was just changed. If you did not make this change, review your security activity at https://myaccount.google.com/security-checkup

red_flags: Hover over the link to make sure it truly goes to myaccount.google.com
analysis.explanation: This looks like a legitimate Google security notification. Google does send alerts when your password is changed. The link points to the real Google security checkup page. If you just changed your password, this is expected. If you did not change it, you should act quickly.
recommended_actions: If you just changed your Google password, you can ignore this
If you did NOT change your password, go to myaccount.google.com immediately and secure your account
Enable two-factor authentication if you have not already
Check your recent security activity at myaccount.google.com/security-checkup
analysis.safe_alternatives: Always go to myaccount.google.com directly in your browser rather than clicking any link in an email, just to be safe.

message: I'm deployed overseas with the Army and I found your profile on ChristianMingle. You seem like such a kind person. I know this is forward, but I feel God has put you in my path. I can't access my bank accounts from here. Could you help me with a $200 iTunes gift card so I can call my daughter for her birthday? I'll pay you back as soon as I'm stateside.

red_flags: Claims to be deployed military (extremely common scam persona)
Cannot access bank accounts (manufactured excuse for needing help)
Requests gift cards (untraceable payment method)
Religious and emotional manipulation
Asks for money from someone they just met online
iTunes gift cards are not used for phone calls
analysis.explanation: This is a romance scam using a fake military persona. Scammers frequently pretend to be deployed soldiers because it explains why they cannot meet in person or video chat. The story about needing gift cards to call their daughter is designed to tug at your heartstrings. Real soldiers have access to their bank accounts and do not need iTunes gift cards to make phone calls. The military provides free communication services to deployed personnel.
recommended_actions: Do NOT buy any gift cards
Do NOT send money to someone you have never met in person
Deployed military personnel have free calling options through the military
Report the profile to ChristianMingle
You can verify military service through official channels
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want to support real military families, donate to established organizations like the USO (uso.org) or Blue Star Families (bluestarfam.org). Never send money or gift cards to someone you have only met online.

message: FINAL NOTICE: Your vehicle warranty is about to expire. This is your last chance to extend coverage before your 2019 Honda CR-V is no longer eligible. Press 1 to speak with a representative now or call 1-866-555-0234.

analysis.scam_type: Advance fee fraud / Vehicle warranty scam
red_flags: Final notice is a classic pressure tactic (they send many final notices)
They may or may not know your actual car -- often a lucky guess or bought data
Legitimate warranty companies do not cold call or text with scare tactics
Will try to sell you a worthless warranty or collect your credit card info
The company is likely not authorized by Honda
Uses urgency to prevent you from researching
analysis.explanation: This is a vehicle warranty scam, one of the most common scams in America. Even if they correctly name your car model, that information is available from public records. The warranty they sell is usually worthless and riddled with exclusions, or they simply steal your credit card information. Real warranty extensions come from your car manufacturer or dealer, not random callers.
recommended_actions: Do NOT press 1 or call the number
Hang up immediately
If you want warranty coverage, contact your Honda dealer directly
Register your number on the Do Not Call list at donotcall.gov
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are genuinely interested in extended warranty coverage, contact your Honda dealership directly or check with your car insurance provider for mechanical breakdown coverage.

message: Your Spotify Premium subscription payment of $10.99 failed. Update your payment method to continue uninterrupted service: https://www.spotify.com/account/subscription/

red_flags: Payment failure notifications are commonly spoofed by scammers
Verify the link actually goes to spotify.com before clicking
analysis.explanation: This could be a legitimate Spotify payment notification or a phishing attempt. The link appears to go to the real Spotify website, but it is impossible to tell from the text alone whether it is a real link or a disguised one. Spotify does send notifications when payments fail. The safest approach is to check directly through the Spotify app or website rather than clicking the link.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link in the email or text
Open the Spotify app on your phone to check your subscription status
Or go to spotify.com directly in your browser and log in
Check your bank statement to see if the Spotify charge went through
analysis.safe_alternatives: Open the Spotify app or go to spotify.com directly and check your account status there. If your payment really failed, you will see a notification in the app.

message: Action required: Your property taxes for 2026 are due by April 15. Amount: $2,847.00. Pay online at tax.gwinnettcounty.com or mail payment to 75 Langley Drive, Lawrenceville, GA 30046. Receipt #PCT-2026-18734.

red_flags: Verify the website domain matches your county's official tax website
Confirm the mailing address matches your county tax office
analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate property tax notice. It includes a specific amount, due date, official county website, and mailing address, which is consistent with how county tax offices communicate. However, it is always wise to verify by checking your county's official website independently.
recommended_actions: Verify the website by searching for your county tax office independently
Compare the amount with your previous tax bills
If everything checks out, pay through the official channel you trust
Keep the receipt number for your records
analysis.safe_alternatives: Search for your county tax assessor website through Google rather than clicking any links. You can also call your county tax office to confirm the amount.

message: Hi, I noticed you're selling the couch on Facebook Marketplace. I'd like to buy it! I'll send you a cashier's check for $850 -- I know it's listed at $400, but the extra is to cover the moving company I'm sending to pick it up. Just deposit the check and pay the movers $450 when they arrive.

red_flags: Offering more than the asking price for no good reason
Sending a cashier's check (will be fake)
Asking you to pay a third party from the check amount
The check will bounce after you have paid the movers with real money
Buyer shows no interest in seeing the item first
Classic overpayment scam pattern
analysis.explanation: This is an overpayment scam. The check for $850 will look real and your bank may even let you deposit it, but it will bounce a few days later. By then, you will have already paid $450 of your own real money to the fake movers (who are part of the scam). You will be out $450 and still have the couch. Nobody pays more than the asking price for used furniture.
recommended_actions: Do NOT accept a check for more than the asking price
Do NOT deposit checks from strangers
Only accept cash or secure in-person payment methods for Marketplace sales
Report the buyer to Facebook Marketplace
If you already deposited a check, contact your bank immediately
analysis.safe_alternatives: For Facebook Marketplace sales, meet in person at a safe public location and accept only cash, Venmo, or other instant payment methods where you can verify the money immediately.

message: We noticed unusual sign-in activity on your Microsoft account. Sign-in from: Moscow, Russia. Device: Unknown. If this was not you, secure your account at: account.live.com/security

red_flags: Sign-in alert emails are frequently spoofed by phishers
Hover over the link to verify it actually goes to account.live.com
Check the sender email address carefully for misspellings
analysis.explanation: This could be a legitimate Microsoft security alert or a phishing attempt. Microsoft does send alerts about unusual sign-in activity, and the link appears to go to the real Microsoft domain. However, these alerts are one of the most commonly faked emails. The safest approach is to go to your Microsoft account directly rather than clicking any link.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link in the email
Go directly to account.microsoft.com in your browser
Check your recent activity under Security settings
If you see a sign-in you do not recognize, change your password immediately
Enable two-factor authentication if not already active
analysis.safe_alternatives: Go to account.microsoft.com directly, sign in, and check your recent activity. If there is a real unauthorized sign-in, you will see it there.

message: YOU HAVE WON! Your phone number was selected in the Mega Millions Second Chance Drawing. Your prize: $175,000. To claim, contact Agent Robert Williams at claims@megamillions-winners.org. You must pay a $500 tax processing fee to release your winnings.

red_flags: You cannot win a lottery you did not enter
There is no Mega Millions Second Chance Drawing
Asking for an upfront fee to claim winnings
Real lottery winnings are never conditional on paying fees first
Email domain megamillions-winners.org is not official
Taxes on real lottery winnings are deducted from the prize, not paid upfront
analysis.explanation: This is a lottery scam. You cannot win a drawing you never entered. There is no such thing as a phone-number-based Mega Millions drawing. The scammers want you to pay $500 to claim the fake prize, and after that, they will keep inventing more fees. You will never receive any money. Real lottery taxes are deducted from your winnings automatically -- you never have to pay to collect.
recommended_actions: Do NOT contact the agent
Do NOT pay any fees
Delete the message immediately
Remember: if you did not buy a ticket, you cannot win
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you play the lottery, results are available at your state lottery's official website. You never need to pay a fee to claim legitimate winnings.

message: PayPal: You sent $329.99 to CryptoTrader_X. If you did not authorize this transaction, call us immediately at 1-888-555-0321 to request a refund. Transaction ID: #PP-38291746.

red_flags: The phone number is not PayPal's real support number
Designed to create panic about a large unauthorized payment
If you call, they will ask for your PayPal login or financial details
PayPal's real support is at 1-888-221-1161
Transaction IDs can be fabricated easily
analysis.explanation: This is a callback phishing scam. The payment described never actually happened. The scammer wants you to panic and call their fake number, where they will pretend to be PayPal support and ask for your login credentials, credit card number, or other personal information. PayPal does send transaction alerts, but you should always check inside the PayPal app rather than calling a number from a text.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number in the message
Open the PayPal app or go to paypal.com to check your real transaction history
If you see no matching transaction, the message is fake
PayPal's real support number is 1-888-221-1161
Report phishing to PayPal at phishing@paypal.com
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into PayPal directly at paypal.com or in the app to check your transactions. If there is no $329.99 charge, the text was fake.

message: Hi, I'm reaching out from the Red Cross about the recent tornado in Alabama. We are collecting donations to help families affected by the disaster. Please donate at redcross.org/donate or text REDCROSS to 90999 to give $10.

red_flags: Verify the link goes to the real redcross.org
The text-to-donate number 90999 is the actual Red Cross donation number
analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate Red Cross donation request. The American Red Cross does run donation campaigns during natural disasters, and the text-to-donate number 90999 is their real shortcode. The website domain redcross.org is the official site. This is consistent with how the Red Cross communicates.
recommended_actions: If you want to donate, you can text REDCROSS to 90999 (this is legitimate)
You can also donate directly at redcross.org
Always verify by going to redcross.org directly rather than clicking links
analysis.safe_alternatives: Go to redcross.org directly in your browser to donate. You can also call 1-800-RED-CROSS (1-800-733-2767) to make a donation by phone.

message: Dear taxpayer, the IRS owes you a refund of $1,247.82 from your 2025 tax return. To expedite processing, verify your identity and direct deposit information at irs-refund-portal.com. Refund Reference: IRS-REF-20260407.

red_flags: The IRS does not send emails or texts about refunds
Fake domain (irs-refund-portal.com is not irs.gov)
The IRS already has your direct deposit info from your tax return
Asking you to verify identity through an unofficial portal
Fake reference number
The IRS uses irs.gov, never any other domain
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing scam impersonating the IRS. The IRS does not contact taxpayers through email, text messages, or social media to request personal or financial information. If you are owed a refund, it will be processed automatically based on the information you provided when you filed. The IRS always uses the irs.gov domain and sends official correspondence through the US mail.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Do NOT enter any personal information
Check your refund status at the real IRS website: irs.gov/refunds
Report the phishing email to phishing@irs.gov
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your refund status at irs.gov/refunds using the Where's My Refund tool. You will need your Social Security number, filing status, and exact refund amount.

message: Your Xfinity bill of $132.47 is due on April 15. Pay at xfinity.com/pay or call 1-800-XFINITY. Auto-pay is active on your Visa ending in 3892.

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard Xfinity bill reminder. It includes a specific amount, due date, official website, and official phone number. The reference to auto-pay with the last four digits of your card is consistent with how Xfinity communicates. If you are an Xfinity customer, this is almost certainly legitimate.
recommended_actions: If you are an Xfinity customer and the amount looks right, no action needed if auto-pay is on
You can verify by logging into your Xfinity account at xfinity.com
If the amount seems wrong, call 1-800-XFINITY to inquire
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into your Xfinity account at xfinity.com to verify your bill amount and payment status.

message: Yo this is your DoorDash driver. Order is delivered but the app glitched and I didn't get my tip. Can you Venmo me the tip directly? My Venmo is @QuickCash2026. Thanks!

red_flags: DoorDash drivers are paid through the app, not Venmo
A driver would never text you from a personal number about tips
The app tip system works reliably
Sending money outside the platform removes all buyer protection
The Venmo handle is generic and suspicious
analysis.explanation: This is a social engineering scam. DoorDash drivers receive tips through the DoorDash app, and the system works reliably. A real driver would not contact you through a personal number to ask for a Venmo payment. Even if there were a legitimate app glitch, the correct fix would be to contact DoorDash support, not to ask customers for Venmo payments.
recommended_actions: Do NOT send money on Venmo
Tips go through the DoorDash app only
Report this message to DoorDash support
Block the phone number
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want to tip your DoorDash driver more, you can adjust the tip in the DoorDash app after delivery. All payments should go through the official app.

message: BREAKING: Work from home and earn $5,000/week! Simply process payments from your home computer. No experience needed. We provide full training. Flexible hours. Apply now at homepayjobs.net

red_flags: $5,000/week for unskilled work is unrealistic
Processing payments is a euphemism for money laundering
No experience needed for high-paying work is a red flag
You could face criminal charges for laundering money
Unverifiable company with no real identity
The website is not associated with any legitimate employer
analysis.explanation: This is a money mule recruitment scam disguised as a job offer. If you accept, they will send stolen money to your bank account and ask you to transfer it elsewhere, keeping a percentage. This is money laundering, and you can be held criminally responsible even if you did not know the money was stolen. The $5,000/week is bait to get you involved in illegal activity.
recommended_actions: Do NOT apply or provide personal information
Do NOT agree to process payments for anyone
Understand that processing payments for strangers is money laundering
Report to the FBI's Internet Crime Complaint Center at ic3.gov
Look for real jobs on Indeed, LinkedIn, or USAJobs.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: Real work-from-home jobs are listed on reputable job boards like Indeed, LinkedIn, FlexJobs, or company career pages. Any job promising $5,000/week with no experience is a scam.

message: Venmo: Sarah Miller paid you $50.00. Note: lunch money, thx! View transaction at venmo.com/transaction/382917463

red_flags: Verify the link goes to venmo.com before clicking
analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard Venmo payment notification. It includes the sender's name, amount, and a note about the payment, which is consistent with how Venmo notifications work. If someone you know named Sarah Miller owes you money, this is very likely legitimate.
recommended_actions: Open the Venmo app to verify the payment appeared in your account
If you do not know a Sarah Miller, check the Venmo app to see who sent the money
Do not click the link -- check directly in the app instead
analysis.safe_alternatives: Always verify payments by opening the Venmo app directly rather than clicking links in notifications.

message: Congratulations on your home purchase! Here are your closing documents from Atlantic Title Company. Please wire your closing costs of $12,480.00 to the following account by April 10: Bank of America, Routing #026009593, Account #483729103847. Contact Tom at tom.reynolds@atlantic-title-services.com with questions.

red_flags: Wire transfer instructions sent via email (extremely common real estate scam)
Real estate wire fraud costs victims an average of $150,000+
Email addresses and accounts can be spoofed to look legitimate
Once wire is sent, the money is nearly impossible to recover
You should NEVER trust wire instructions received via email alone
analysis.explanation: This may be a real estate wire fraud scam, which is one of the most devastating scams today. Hackers break into real estate agents' or title companies' email accounts and send fake wire instructions to home buyers. The money goes to the scammer's account and is usually gone within hours. Even if you are actually buying a home, you should NEVER follow wire instructions from an email without verifying by phone.
recommended_actions: Do NOT wire any money based on this email alone
Call your title company at the number from your original paperwork (NOT from this email)
Verbally confirm wire instructions over the phone
Ask your real estate agent to verify the instructions in person
If you already wired money to unverified instructions, call your bank immediately
analysis.safe_alternatives: Always call your title company using a phone number you obtained independently (not from the email) to verify wire transfer instructions before sending any money.

message: URGENT MESSAGE FROM SHERIFF'S DEPARTMENT: You failed to appear for jury duty on March 28. A bench warrant has been issued for your arrest. To resolve this matter and avoid arrest, call 404-555-0187 immediately. You may settle the fine of $1,200 by phone today.

red_flags: Courts do not call or text about missed jury duty -- they send official letters
Real warrants are not resolved by phone payment
The phone number is not associated with any real sheriff's office
Fines for missed jury duty are handled through the court system, not phone calls
Creates extreme fear of arrest to prevent rational thinking
analysis.explanation: This is a government impersonation scam. Courts handle missed jury duty through official written notices, not phone calls demanding immediate payment. A real bench warrant would not come with a phone number to call and pay a fine. The scammer wants you to panic about being arrested so you will pay quickly without thinking.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
Do NOT pay any money
If you are genuinely concerned about jury duty, contact your local courthouse directly
Look up your county court's real phone number online
Report to your local police department
analysis.safe_alternatives: Contact your county courthouse directly using the phone number on their official government website. If you truly missed jury duty, they will explain the proper process for resolving it.

message: Hey it's been a while! I found some old photos of us from college and uploaded them here: bit.ly/collegephotos2026. You look so different haha!

red_flags: Vague message that could apply to anyone
Shortened URL hides the real destination
Classic social engineering tactic -- curiosity about photos
The link likely leads to a phishing page or malware download
No specific name or identifying details about the sender
College photos is a common lure because it appeals to nostalgia
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing message that uses curiosity and nostalgia as bait. The message is intentionally vague so it could apply to almost anyone. The shortened link likely goes to a fake login page (to steal your password) or a website that downloads malware to your device. A real friend would use your name and provide more specific context.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Ask the sender a specific question only a real friend would know
If you do not recognize the number, block it
If this came from a friend's account, their account may be hacked -- contact them through another method
Report the message as spam
analysis.safe_alternatives: If a friend genuinely wants to share photos, they would use a service you both know (like Google Photos, iCloud, or Facebook) and they would mention specific details that prove they know you.

message: This is a fraud alert from Bank of America. A $1,247.00 purchase was attempted at Walmart in Phoenix, AZ using your debit card. Was this you? Reply YES or NO.

red_flags: Fraud alert texts are frequently spoofed
Replying YES or NO may confirm your number is active
Real Bank of America fraud alerts come from short codes, not regular numbers
However, banks do send legitimate fraud alerts in this format
analysis.explanation: This could be a real Bank of America fraud alert or a scam. Real banks do send text alerts about suspicious transactions. However, scammers also send fake fraud alerts to start a conversation that leads to stealing your information. The safest approach is to not reply to the text and instead call Bank of America directly.
recommended_actions: Do NOT reply to the text
Call Bank of America directly at 1-800-432-1000 (the number on the back of your card)
Log into your Bank of America app to check recent transactions
If you see the charge, report it as unauthorized through the app or phone
If you do not see the charge, the text was fake
analysis.safe_alternatives: Call 1-800-432-1000 (Bank of America's official fraud line) or check your transactions in the Bank of America mobile app.

message: ATTENTION: You have been chosen to participate in a government-funded solar panel program. Get solar panels installed on your home for $0 down. This offer is only available this week. Call 1-800-555-0276 to schedule your free installation.

red_flags: There is no government program that gives free solar panels
Extreme urgency (this week only) is a pressure tactic
Cold calls and texts about free solar panels are typically misleading
The $0 down often hides expensive long-term contracts or liens on your home
You may end up locked into a 20-25 year lease or loan
analysis.explanation: While solar panels can be a good investment, this message is misleading at best and potentially a scam. There is no government program giving away free solar panels. What they are usually selling is a solar lease or loan with $0 upfront, but with long-term payments and potential liens on your home. Some of these companies use high-pressure tactics and hide unfavorable terms in the fine print.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
If you are interested in solar, research independently
Get quotes from multiple established solar companies
Check the federal solar tax credit at energy.gov
Read all contracts carefully, especially the fine print about lease terms and liens
Check company reviews on BBB.org before signing anything
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are genuinely interested in solar panels, get quotes from at least three established companies. Check energysage.com for unbiased comparisons. The real federal solar tax credit is a 30% tax credit, not free panels.

message: Auto-payment received: $89.99 charged to your Amex ending in 1004 for your Verizon Wireless bill. Thank you. Next bill date: May 7. View account: verizon.com/signin

analysis.explanation: This is a standard autopayment confirmation from Verizon Wireless. It includes the specific amount, the last four digits of the payment card, the next bill date, and a link to the real Verizon website. Wireless carriers routinely send these payment confirmations.
recommended_actions: If you are a Verizon customer and this amount looks correct, no action needed
You can verify by logging into verizon.com or the My Verizon app
If the amount seems incorrect, call Verizon at 1-800-922-0204
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your Verizon account at verizon.com or in the My Verizon app to confirm the payment.

message: I'm sorry to bother you, but I'm stuck at the airport and my wallet was stolen. I'm a pastor from First Baptist Church in Tulsa and I just need $150 for a bus ticket home. I can send you the money back through my church as soon as I get there. God bless you.

analysis.scam_type: Advance fee fraud / Stranded traveler scam
red_flags: Stranger asking for money with a sympathetic story
Claims authority figure status (pastor) to build trust
Promises to pay you back through a vague channel
Stranded traveler stories are an extremely common scam
Emotional manipulation through religious appeal
A real stranded person would contact their church or family, not strangers
analysis.explanation: This is a stranded traveler scam. The person is not really a pastor and is not really stuck at an airport. This story is designed to appeal to your kindness and generosity. A real person in this situation would call their family, their church, or use services designed to help stranded travelers. They would not approach strangers for cash.
recommended_actions: Do NOT give money to strangers with stories like this
If you want to help, offer to call a specific organization for them
Suggest they contact the airport police or information desk
A real church would wire their pastor money -- suggest they call their church
Walk away if they become insistent or aggressive
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you encounter someone who seems genuinely stranded, you can help by directing them to airport information desk staff, police, or suggesting they call 211 (community assistance helpline).

message: Your Amazon Prime membership payment was declined. Your membership benefits will be suspended within 24 hours unless you update your payment at: amazon-prime-billing-update.com/verify

red_flags: Fake domain (amazon-prime-billing-update.com is not amazon.com)
24-hour deadline creates artificial urgency
Amazon sends payment issues through the Amazon app, not random emails
The link will steal your Amazon login and credit card info
No mention of your name or specific account details
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing scam pretending to be Amazon. The website is not Amazon -- it is a fake page designed to steal your login credentials and credit card information. Real Amazon payment notifications appear in the Amazon app and on the actual amazon.com website. They never use third-party domains.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Log into amazon.com directly to check your Prime membership
If your payment genuinely failed, Amazon will show a banner when you log in
Report the email to stop-spoofing@amazon.com
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: Go directly to amazon.com, log in, and check your membership status under Account > Prime Membership. If there is a real payment issue, you will see it there.

message: Your child's school photo proofs are ready! View and order prints at lifetouch.com/order. Use access code: SPRING2026-RHS. Orders due by April 18.

analysis.explanation: This looks like a legitimate school photo notification from Lifetouch, which is the largest school photography company in the US. It includes a specific access code and deadline, which is consistent with how Lifetouch communicates with parents. The website lifetouch.com is the real company website.
recommended_actions: If your child recently had school photos taken, this is likely legitimate
Verify by checking with your child or the school office
Visit lifetouch.com to view the proofs using the access code
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can also contact your child's school directly to confirm school photos were recently taken and that Lifetouch is their provider.

message: Hi, I'm calling from the Windows Technical Department. Our system detected that your computer is sending error reports to our server. Your computer has been infected with a serious virus. I need remote access to fix this for you right now. It will cost $299 for the repair.

red_flags: There is no Windows Technical Department
Microsoft never calls customers about computer errors
Requesting remote access is a major red flag
Charging $299 for a fake problem
They cannot detect issues on your specific computer
This is a well-known scam that has robbed millions of people
analysis.explanation: This is a tech support scam. There is no such thing as the Windows Technical Department. Microsoft does not monitor individual computers for errors and will never call you to say your computer is infected. If you give them remote access, they will either install malware, lock your files for ransom, or simply charge you for doing nothing. The $299 fee is for a problem that does not exist.
recommended_actions: Hang up immediately
Do NOT give anyone remote access to your computer
Do NOT pay anything
If you already gave remote access, disconnect from the internet immediately
Change all your passwords from a different device
Run Windows Defender to scan for anything they may have installed
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you think your computer has a problem, take it to a local repair shop you trust or use the built-in Windows Defender antivirus which is free. You can also visit a Microsoft Store for help.

message: EXCLUSIVE INVITATION: Join our private investment club. We use insider trading algorithms to guarantee 300% annual returns. Minimum investment $10,000. Current members include Fortune 500 CEOs. Wire funds to our Cayman Islands account to get started. Spots limited.

red_flags: Guaranteed returns (no legitimate investment can guarantee returns)
300% annual returns is absurd and impossible to sustain
Mentions insider trading, which is illegal
Cayman Islands account (offshore to avoid regulation)
Claims about Fortune 500 CEO members with no proof
Wire transfer to offshore account is irreversible
Artificial scarcity with limited spots
analysis.explanation: This is an investment scam that may be a Ponzi scheme. Guaranteed 300% returns do not exist. The mention of insider trading is not a selling point -- it is a federal crime. The Cayman Islands account ensures your money is beyond the reach of US law enforcement. Everything about this screams fraud. Even the most successful investors in history have never achieved consistent 300% annual returns.
recommended_actions: Do NOT invest any money
Do NOT wire funds anywhere
Report to the SEC at sec.gov/tcr
Report to the FBI at ic3.gov
Block the sender
If you already sent money, contact your bank and law enforcement immediately
analysis.safe_alternatives: Invest only through regulated brokerages like Fidelity, Schwab, or Vanguard. If anyone guarantees returns, especially high ones, it is a scam. The S&P 500 has averaged about 10% annually over decades -- that is reality.

message: State Farm Insurance: Your auto policy renewal documents are available. Annual premium: $1,847.00, effective May 1 through April 30. View policy: statefarm.com/customer/policy-documents. Questions? Call your agent Emily at 770-555-0134.

analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate auto insurance renewal notification from State Farm. It includes a specific premium amount, coverage dates, a link to the real State Farm website, and a named agent with a phone number. Insurance companies routinely send renewal notices like this.
recommended_actions: If you are a State Farm customer, review your renewal documents
Compare your premium to last year's to check for increases
If you want to shop around, get quotes from other insurers before your renewal date
Call your agent if you have questions about coverage
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into your State Farm account at statefarm.com to view your full policy documents and verify the premium amount.

message: HELP! I'm a Nigerian prince and my family's $47 million fortune is frozen by the government. I need to move the money to an American bank account temporarily. I will give you $5 million for helping. Just send me $2,000 for the initial legal fees and I will send you the contract. God will bless you abundantly.

red_flags: Nigerian prince scam is perhaps the most famous scam in history
Stranger offering millions for minimal help
Requires upfront payment (the advance fee)
Religious language used to build false trust
No Nigerian prince has ever distributed millions via email
The fees will escalate and the money will never arrive
analysis.explanation: This is the classic Nigerian prince email scam, also known as a 419 scam (named after the Nigerian criminal code). It has been around since the 1980s and has stolen billions of dollars from victims worldwide. There is no prince, no fortune, and no $5 million reward. If you send $2,000, they will ask for more and more, inventing new fees each time. Many victims have lost their life savings.
recommended_actions: Do NOT respond
Do NOT send any money
Mark as spam and delete
Report to the FBI at ic3.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: No legitimate financial arrangement begins with a stranger asking you to pay upfront fees. Delete and move on.

message: Reminder: Your annual physical is scheduled for April 14 at 9:00 AM with Dr. Patel at Piedmont Healthcare. Please bring your insurance card and arrive 10 minutes early. Reply YES to confirm or call 770-555-0198 to reschedule.

analysis.explanation: This is a standard medical appointment reminder. It includes a specific doctor name, healthcare system, date, time, and practical instructions about what to bring. Piedmont Healthcare is a major healthcare provider in Georgia. Medical offices routinely send automated appointment reminders like this.
recommended_actions: If you have this appointment scheduled, reply to confirm
Bring your insurance card as requested
If you need to reschedule, call the number provided
If you do not recognize this appointment, call the office to clarify
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are unsure, call Piedmont Healthcare directly through their main number on their website to verify the appointment.

message: Hi, I noticed a charge of $49.99 on your Visa card for an Adult Entertainment Subscription. If you did not make this purchase, click here to dispute it immediately: visa-dispute-center.net

red_flags: Fake domain (visa-dispute-center.net is not a real Visa site)
Uses shame and embarrassment to pressure quick action
Designed to make you click before thinking (especially if others might see)
Visa does not send charge notifications -- your bank does
The link will steal your credit card information
analysis.explanation: This is a shame-based phishing scam. The scammers mention an embarrassing charge to make you panic and click the link quickly before anyone else sees it, especially if you are around family. The link goes to a fake website that will steal your Visa card number and personal information. Visa itself does not send text notifications about charges -- your bank handles that.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Visa does not send charge notifications, your issuing bank does
Check your real credit card statement through your bank's app
If you see an unauthorized charge, call the number on the back of your card
Report this phishing attempt to your email provider
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your credit card statement through your bank's app or website. If there is a charge you do not recognize, call the number on the back of your physical credit card.

message: Your order from Chewy has shipped! Order #8834721 -- Blue Buffalo Life Protection Formula, 30lb bag. Estimated delivery: April 9-11. Track at chewy.com/orders

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard Chewy shipping confirmation. It includes a specific order number, product name, delivery window, and a link to the real chewy.com domain. Chewy is a major pet supply retailer that sends these notifications routinely.
recommended_actions: If you ordered from Chewy recently, this is legitimate
Track your package at chewy.com
If you did not order this, log into your Chewy account to check for unauthorized orders
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into your Chewy account at chewy.com to verify the order and track your shipment.

message: You've received a secure fax from the IRS regarding your tax account. Download your document at irs-secure-fax.com/download/doc-2026-0407. The document contains sensitive tax information and will expire in 48 hours.

red_flags: The IRS does not send secure faxes via email or text
Fake domain (irs-secure-fax.com is not irs.gov)
Downloading the document will likely install malware
48-hour deadline creates false urgency
The IRS communicates through US mail, not digital fax links
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing scam that will likely install malware on your computer if you download the document. The IRS does not send digital faxes through email links. Any official IRS communication comes through the United States Postal Service. The downloaded file could be ransomware, a keylogger, or other malicious software that steals your data.
recommended_actions: Do NOT download the document
Do NOT click the link
Report to phishing@irs.gov
If you accidentally downloaded it, disconnect from the internet and run a full antivirus scan
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: The IRS only communicates through physical mail. If you need to access IRS documents, go to irs.gov and log into your account there.

message: Duke Energy: Outage alert for your area (30062). Crews are working to restore power. Estimated restoration: 6:00 PM today. Updates: duke-energy.com/outages or call 800-419-6356.

analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate outage notification from Duke Energy. It includes a specific zip code, estimated restoration time, and links to the real Duke Energy website and their real customer service number. Utility companies routinely send outage alerts to affected customers.
recommended_actions: If you are a Duke Energy customer and your power is out, this confirms crews are working on it
Check duke-energy.com/outages for live updates
If you did not lose power, you can ignore this
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can check the Duke Energy outage map at duke-energy.com/outages to verify if there is a real outage in your area.

message: Congratulations! Your business has been selected for a Google Guaranteed listing. This premium placement puts you at the very top of Google search results. To activate your listing, pay the one-time verification fee of $499 at google-guaranteed-listings.com. This offer expires in 24 hours.

red_flags: Fake domain (google-guaranteed-listings.com is not google.com)
Google Guaranteed is part of Google Local Services and is NOT sold this way
One-time fee of $499 is not how Google Guaranteed works
24-hour deadline is a pressure tactic
Google does not select businesses for premium listings via email
The real Google Guaranteed program requires background checks, not payments to random websites
analysis.explanation: This is a scam targeting business owners. Google Guaranteed is a real program, but it is part of Google Local Services Ads and you sign up through ads.google.com. Google never emails businesses offering premium placement for a fee. The link goes to a fake website that will steal your credit card information or payment.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link or pay any money
The real Google Guaranteed program is at ads.google.com/local-services-ads
Google never charges a one-time fee for search placement
Report to Google at safebrowsing.google.com/safebrowsing/report_phish
Delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are interested in Google Guaranteed, visit ads.google.com/local-services-ads to learn about the real program and apply through official channels.

message: Hi, I represent a casting agency and we found your daughter's photo on Instagram. She has a real look for modeling. We'd love to bring her in for a portfolio shoot. There is a standard $350 portfolio fee. Can you bring her to our studio this Saturday?

red_flags: Legitimate agencies do not charge upfront portfolio fees
Unsolicited contact through social media is a red flag
Real modeling agencies make money from booking work, not from charging talent
Flattery is used to lower your guard
Targets parents through their children
No verifiable agency name or credentials provided
analysis.explanation: This is a talent scam. Legitimate modeling and casting agencies do not charge upfront fees for portfolios. They make their money by booking paid work and taking a commission. This scammer is trying to charge you $350 for photos that will lead nowhere. Worse, it could be a setup for a dangerous situation involving your child.
recommended_actions: Do NOT pay any fees
Do NOT bring your child to an unverified location
Legitimate agencies do not scout on Instagram and charge portfolio fees
If you are interested in modeling for your child, research reputable agencies in your area
Report this account on Instagram
Consider contacting local police if they persist
analysis.safe_alternatives: Real modeling agencies like Ford, Wilhelmina, and IMG do not charge upfront fees. If your child is interested in modeling, research established agencies and apply through their official websites.

message: GEORGIA POWER: Your account is past due. Amount: $247.83. Service will be disconnected on April 10 unless payment is received. Pay at georgiapower.com or call 888-660-5890. Account ending: 7234.

red_flags: Verify the phone number matches Georgia Power's real number (888-660-5890 is correct)
Past-due notices are also commonly spoofed, so verify independently
analysis.explanation: This appears to be a legitimate past-due notice from Georgia Power. The phone number 888-660-5890 is Georgia Power's real customer service number, and the link goes to the correct website. Georgia Power does send disconnection warnings via text for past-due accounts. The inclusion of the last four digits of your account number is consistent with their communication style.
recommended_actions: If you are a Georgia Power customer, check your account status at georgiapower.com
Call 888-660-5890 (Georgia Power's real number) to discuss payment options
Georgia Power offers payment plans if you are having trouble paying
If your account is current, this may be a spoofed message -- verify directly
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into your Georgia Power account at georgiapower.com to check your balance and payment status.

message: Dear homeowner, are you struggling with mortgage payments? The Federal Homeowner Relief Program is offering $0 mortgage payments for 6 months. You've been pre-qualified! Call 1-800-555-0349 to activate your relief. Must respond within 72 hours.

red_flags: There is no Federal Homeowner Relief Program by this name
You cannot be pre-qualified for something you never applied for
72-hour deadline is a pressure tactic
The phone number leads to a scam operation
They will likely ask for your mortgage details, Social Security number, and upfront fees
Targets people in financial distress
analysis.explanation: This is a mortgage relief scam. There is no government program by this name offering $0 mortgage payments. If you call, they will collect your personal information and may charge you fees for services that are actually available for free through your mortgage servicer or through HUD-approved housing counselors. Some of these scams also steal your home through fraudulent deed transfers.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number
Do NOT share your mortgage or personal information
If you need mortgage help, contact your loan servicer directly
Call a HUD-approved housing counselor for free help: 1-800-569-4287
Visit hud.gov for legitimate homeowner assistance programs
Report to the FTC at reportfraud.ftc.gov
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are struggling with mortgage payments, call your mortgage servicer directly and ask about forbearance or modification options. You can also get free help from a HUD-approved housing counselor at 1-800-569-4287.

message: GitHub notification: A pull request was merged in forge-dev/studio. PR #247: Update blog post templates by corey-forge. View at github.com/forge-dev/studio/pull/247

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard GitHub notification about a merged pull request. It includes a specific repository name, PR number, description, and contributor name. If you use GitHub for work or personal projects, these notifications are routine.
recommended_actions: If you use this GitHub repository, this is a normal notification
Click through to review the changes if needed
Adjust your GitHub notification settings if you are receiving too many
analysis.safe_alternatives: You can view your GitHub notifications directly at github.com/notifications.

message: Hey, I'm an artist and I'd love to paint your pet! I saw your dog on Instagram and he's perfect for my portrait series. I just need you to pay $25 for shipping supplies upfront, and I'll send you the finished portrait for free. Venmo me at @PetArtist2026!

analysis.scam_type: Advance fee fraud / Social media scam
red_flags: Unsolicited offer from a stranger on social media
Asks for upfront payment before any work is done
Free portrait but you pay for shipping supplies upfront
Venmo payments are hard to reverse
No portfolio or verifiable identity provided
analysis.explanation: This is likely a small-scale advance fee scam on social media. While the amount is small ($25), you will likely never receive a portrait. The scammer sends this message to thousands of pet owners, collects $25 from each one who bites, and disappears. Even if it is not a scam, paying strangers on Venmo for unverified services is risky.
recommended_actions: Do NOT send money to unknown people on Venmo
If interested in a pet portrait, ask to see their portfolio first
Search for their business on Google and check reviews
Only pay after receiving the finished product, or use a payment method with buyer protection
Be skeptical of unsolicited offers from strangers
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you want a pet portrait, find established artists on Etsy, where you have buyer protection, or look for local artists with verifiable reviews and portfolios.

message: Booking.com confirmation: Your reservation at Hilton Garden Inn Atlanta Midtown is confirmed. Check-in: April 18, Check-out: April 20. Confirmation #BC-83927461. Manage reservation: booking.com/myreservation

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard hotel reservation confirmation from Booking.com. It includes specific hotel name, dates, and a confirmation number. The link points to the real booking.com domain. If you recently made a hotel reservation, this is likely legitimate.
recommended_actions: If you booked this hotel, save the confirmation number for check-in
Verify by logging into your Booking.com account at booking.com
If you did not make this reservation, log into Booking.com to check for unauthorized bookings and change your password
analysis.safe_alternatives: Log into your Booking.com account directly to view and manage your reservations.

message: CONSUMER NOTICE: Your credit score has dropped 47 points this month. A derogatory mark has been reported to TransUnion. Fix your credit NOW before it gets worse. Free credit repair consultation at credit-fix-pro.com. Call 1-877-555-0293.

red_flags: Credit bureaus do not send unsolicited texts about score drops
Scare tactic about a 47-point drop to create urgency
Credit repair companies that cold-contact you are usually scams
They will charge you for services you can do yourself for free
The website is not affiliated with TransUnion or any credit bureau
It is illegal to charge upfront fees for credit repair under federal law
analysis.explanation: This is a credit repair scam. TransUnion and other credit bureaus do not send texts about score drops. The scammers want you to panic about your credit and sign up for expensive credit repair services that either do nothing or do things you could do for free yourself. Under the Credit Repair Organizations Act, it is illegal to charge upfront fees for credit repair, but these companies often do it anyway.
recommended_actions: Do NOT call the number or visit the website
Check your real credit score for free at annualcreditreport.com
You can dispute credit report errors yourself for free at each bureau's website
If you need help, contact a HUD-approved credit counselor at 1-800-569-4287
Report credit repair scams to the FTC
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your real credit score for free through your bank's app, Credit Karma (creditkarma.com), or annualcreditreport.com. You can dispute errors directly with TransUnion, Equifax, and Experian at no cost.

message: Your Uber ride receipt: April 6, 2026. Trip from Hartsfield-Jackson Airport to 145 Peachtree St. $34.72 charged to Visa ending 8821. Rate your driver at uber.com/rate

analysis.explanation: This looks like a standard Uber ride receipt. It includes specific trip details (date, pickup, drop-off), the fare amount, and payment card info. Uber routinely sends these receipts after each ride. If you recently took this Uber ride, this is legitimate.
recommended_actions: If you took this ride, this is your receipt -- no action needed
If you did not take this ride, log into your Uber account and check your trip history
Contact Uber support through the app if you see unauthorized trips
analysis.safe_alternatives: Check your trip history in the Uber app to verify this ride matches your records.

message: FINAL WARNING: Your email account will be terminated in 12 hours due to unusual activity. Verify your account immediately at mail-verification-center.com/verify or lose all your emails forever. This is an automated security measure.

red_flags: Fake domain (mail-verification-center.com is not your email provider)
12-hour deadline creates panic
Threatening permanent loss of all emails
No email provider terminates accounts this way
Generic message with no provider name or personal details
Automated security measure language to sound official
analysis.explanation: This is a phishing scam designed to steal your email password. No legitimate email provider (Gmail, Outlook, Yahoo) will threaten to delete your account via an email with a 12-hour deadline. The link goes to a fake page that will capture your email and password when you type them in. Once they have your email password, they can access all your other accounts through password resets.
recommended_actions: Do NOT click the link
Do NOT enter your email or password anywhere
Your email account is fine -- this is a scare tactic
If concerned, go directly to your email provider's website and log in
Report as phishing and delete the message
analysis.safe_alternatives: If you are genuinely worried about your email account, go directly to gmail.com, outlook.com, or whichever email service you use. Log in there to verify everything is fine.
